steps.title: Spot the singular antecedent
steps.content: The blank needs a subject pronoun for the main clause. That pronoun must refer back to "a given term," which is singular.

steps.title: Apply pronoun-antecedent agreement
steps.content:
Identify the grammar issue: the antecedent is "a given term" (singular, non-human). After the parenthetical aside, the blank refers back to this antecedent. Rule: pronoun must agree in number (singular) and demonstrative form must match a singular antecedent.
  • Choice A — "this often becomes": this as a stand-alone pronoun is vague (this what?); it doesn't refer back to "a given term" with appropriate clarity. ✗
  • Choice B — "it often becomes": it is the singular pronoun matching "a given term"; "becomes" agrees with the singular subject. ✓
  • Choice C — "they often become": they is plural — doesn't agree with the singular "a given term." ✗
  • Choice D — "these often become": these is plural — same agreement error as C. ✗
That matches choice B — B is correct.
